agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is architecturally well-suited for this loan application audit requirement — the compliance needs (immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, field-level change tracking) are precisely what event sourcing delivers by design. However, event sourcing introduces significant operational and developmental complexity (event versioning, schema evolution, CQRS projections, eventual consistency) that must be weighed against simpler alternatives. My recommendation is a **hybrid approach**: event sourcing for the loan application lifecycle (where the audit trail IS the domain), paired with CQRS read models for query efficiency. If team maturity or timeline is a constraint, a pragmatic fallback is bi-temporal tables with an append-only audit log, which satisfies compliance at lower complexity cost.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Loan Command Service","responsibility":"Accepts commands that mutate loan application state (SubmitApplication, UpdateIncome, ChangeStatus, etc.). Validates business rules against current state, then emits domain events. Never stores current state directly — state is derived from the event stream.","boundaries":"Owns write path only. No direct queries. Communicates via domain events. Bounded by the loan application aggregate."},{"name":"Event Store","responsibility":"Append-only, immutable persistence of all domain events. Each event is sequenced per aggregate (loan application ID) with a global position. Supports optimistic concurrency via expected version. This IS the system of record — not a side effect.","boundaries":"Infrastructure component. No business logic. Provides stream-per-aggregate and global ordered stream. Must guarantee durability, ordering, and immutability. No UPDATE or DELETE operations permitted."},{"name":"Loan Query Service (Read Model)","responsibility":"Maintains denormalized projections of current loan state, built by subscribing to the event stream. Serves all read/query traffic: application status lookups, dashboard views, search. Projections are disposable and rebuildable from events.","boundaries":"Owns read path only. Eventually consistent with write side. Can have multiple projections optimized for different query patterns."},{"name":"Audit Projection Service","responsibility":"Specialized projection that builds the compliance-oriented audit trail view: who changed what field, when, what the before/after values were. Supports point-in-time state reconstruction by replaying events up to a target timestamp. Serves compliance and regulatory query needs.","boundaries":"Read-only. Subscribes to the same event stream. May store data in a format optimized for audit queries (e.g., field-level changelog, temporal snapshots). Independently deployable from the main query service."},{"name":"Snapshot Service","responsibility":"Periodically captures aggregate state snapshots to optimize replay performance. A loan with hundreds of events over its lifecycle can be reconstituted from the nearest snapshot + subsequent events rather than full replay.","boundaries":"Performance optimization only. Never authoritative — events remain the source of truth. Snapshots can be deleted and regenerated without data loss."},{"name":"Identity Context (External)","responsibility":"Provides authenticated user identity (user ID, role, name) that gets embedded into every command and consequently every event. The 'who' in the audit trail.","boundaries":"Upstream generic context. Loan system is a conformist — accepts identity claims as provided."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Loan Command Service","contract":"HTTP POST commands: SubmitLoanApplication, UpdateApplicantDetails, ChangeLoanStatus, AddDocument, etc. Each command includes authenticated user context (userId, role, timestamp). Returns command acceptance (202) or validation rejection (400/409).","coupling_type":"synchronous, loose (command envelope pattern)"},{"from":"Loan Command Service","to":"Event Store","contract":"Append(streamId, expectedVersion, events[]). Each event: {eventType, aggregateId, version, timestamp, userId, payload}. Optimistic concurrency enforced via expectedVersion. Returns success or concurrency conflict.","coupling_type":"synchronous, tight (infrastructure dependency)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Loan Query Service","contract":"Subscription-based: catch-up subscription delivering ordered events. Query service maintains a checkpoint (last processed position). Events delivered at-least-once; projections must be idempotent.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, loose (pub-sub via event stream)"},{"from":"Event Store","to":"Audit Projection Service","contract":"Same subscription mechanism. Audit projection processes events to build field-level changelogs and temporal state tables. Supports ReconstructStateAt(aggregateId, timestamp) queries.","coupling_type":"asynchronous, loose (pub-sub via event stream)"},{"from":"Loan Query Service","to":"API Gateway","contract":"HTTP GET queries: GetLoanApplication(id), SearchApplications(criteria), GetDashboard(). Returns current projected state. Clients must tolerate eventual consistency (typically sub-second).","coupling_type":"synchronous, loose"},{"from":"Audit Projection Service","to":"Compliance API","contract":"HTTP GET: GetAuditTrail(loanId), GetStateAtPointInTime(loanId, timestamp), GetFieldChangeHistory(loanId, fieldName). Immutable responses. Used by compliance team and regulatory auditors.","coupling_type":"synchronous, loose"}],"data_flow":"1. User submits a command (e.g., UpdateApplicantIncome) via API → 2. Loan Command Service loads current aggregate state by replaying events from Event Store (or from latest snapshot + subsequent events) → 3. Command handler validates business rules against current state → 4. If valid, new domain event(s) emitted (e.g., ApplicantIncomeUpdated{loanId, userId, oldIncome, newIncome, timestamp}) → 5. Events appended to Event Store (immutable, append-only) → 6. Event subscriptions push events to Loan Query Service and Audit Projection Service asynchronously → 7. Projections update their read models → 8. Queries served from read models. For point-in-time reconstruction: Audit Projection replays events for a given aggregate up to the requested timestamp, producing the exact state at that moment.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event Sourcing","rationale":"The compliance requirement IS event sourcing's raison d'être. Every state change is captured as an immutable event with actor, timestamp, and payload. Point-in-time reconstruction is native — just replay to a timestamp. The audit trail is not a bolt-on; it's the fundamental storage model. This eliminates the category of bug where audit and state diverge."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)","rationale":"Event sourcing makes direct queries expensive (must replay events). CQRS separates the write model (event stream) from read models (projections). This allows optimizing query patterns independently — the compliance team gets audit-optimized views while operations gets dashboard-optimized views, both from the same event stream."},{"pattern":"Aggregate Pattern (DDD)","rationale":"Each loan application is an aggregate with a clear consistency boundary. All mutations go through the aggregate root, which enforces business invariants before emitting events. This prevents invalid state transitions (e.g., approving an already-rejected loan) and ensures every event represents a valid domain transition."},{"pattern":"Domain Events","rationale":"Events like LoanSubmitted, CreditCheckCompleted, ApplicantIncomeUpdated capture business intent, not just data diffs. This gives the audit trail semantic meaning — regulators see 'loan was moved to underwriting review by user X' not just 'status column changed from A to B'."},{"pattern":"Snapshotting","rationale":"Long-lived loan applications may accumulate hundreds of events. Periodic snapshots prevent aggregate rehydration from becoming a performance bottleneck. Snapshots are an optimization, not the source of truth."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Loan Application Lifecycle","classification":"core","aggregates":["LoanApplication — Root aggregate. Identity: LoanApplicationId. Manages full lifecycle from submission through underwriting, approval/rejection, disbursement. Enforces invariants: valid status transitions, required documents per stage, data completeness rules. Emits domain events for every state change. Fields include: applicant details, income, employment, requested amount, status, assigned underwriter, decision rationale."]},{"name":"Audit & Compliance","classification":"core","aggregates":["AuditTrail — Read model aggregate (not a write-side aggregate). Built from Loan Application events. Stores field-level change history, actor attribution, and supports temporal queries. No business logic — pure projection. Classification as core because regulatory compliance is non-negotiable for a financial services platform; failure here means fines or license revocation."]},{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User — External context providing userId, role, permissions. The loan system embeds user identity into commands/events but does not own user management."]},{"name":"Document Management","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Document — Supporting context for managing uploaded documents (pay stubs, tax returns, etc.) associated with loan applications. Documents have their own lifecycle but are referenced by the loan aggregate."]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","relationship":"Conformist — Loan context accepts user identity claims as-is from the identity provider. No translation layer needed; user IDs and roles are embedded directly into commands and events."},{"upstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","downstream":"Audit & Compliance","relationship":"Published Language — Loan context publishes a well-defined set of domain events that the Audit context consumes. The event schema IS the published language. Audit context must not require changes to the loan context's event schema to function."},{"upstream":"Document Management","downstream":"Loan Application Lifecycle","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Loan context needs document references and metadata. Document context provides an API. Loan context can request features (e.g., document verification status) but document context controls its own model and release schedule."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Full event sourcing over simpler audit table pattern","gains":"Audit trail is the source of truth, not a derivative — eliminates divergence bugs. Point-in-time reconstruction is native. Events capture business intent. System can evolve to support new projections without schema migration. Event replay enables powerful debugging and analytics.","sacrifices":"Significant complexity increase: event versioning and schema evolution require careful handling. Team needs to learn ES/CQRS patterns. Eventual consistency between write and read models requires UI accommodation. Operational complexity: event store management, projection rebuilds, subscription monitoring.","alternatives_considered":["Bi-temporal tables (system-versioned tables with valid-time and transaction-time columns). Satisfies audit requirements with standard SQL. Point-in-time queries use AS OF syntax. Much simpler operationally. Loses: business intent capture, event-driven architecture benefits, projection flexibility.","Append-only audit log table with triggers. Current state in loan_applications table, triggers capture before/after on every UPDATE to an immutable audit_log table. Simplest to implement. Loses: intent capture (only sees data diffs), harder point-in-time reconstruction (must walk changelog backwards), trigger-based approaches can be brittle and have performance implications.","Change Data Capture (CDC) via Debezium on PostgreSQL WAL. Non-invasive — captures changes at the database level without application changes. Loses: business context (only sees row-level changes, not domain intent), coupled to database schema, CDC infrastructure adds operational burden."]},{"decision":"CQRS with dedicated projections over single-model queries","gains":"Read models can be independently optimized for different access patterns (operational dashboards vs compliance audit). Projections are disposable and rebuildable. Scales read and write independently.","sacrifices":"Multiple data stores to maintain. Eventual consistency requires careful UX design (read-your-own-writes patterns or polling). More moving parts to monitor and debug.","alternatives_considered":["Query the event stream directly for reads. Simpler but unacceptably slow for any non-trivial query pattern. Fine for single-aggregate lookups, terrible for search/filtering/reporting."]},{"decision":"Aggregate-per-loan-application granularity","gains":"Clear consistency boundary. Each loan application's event stream is independent — no cross-application transaction concerns. Simple concurrency model (optimistic locking per stream). Natural partitioning for scale.","sacrifices":"Cross-application queries (e.g., 'all applications by this borrower') must go through read models, not the event store. Sagas needed if business processes span multiple applications.","alternatives_considered":["Larger aggregate (e.g., Borrower aggregate containing all their applications). Rejected: would serialize all operations for a borrower and create unnecessarily large event streams."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Read model projections","evolution_path":"New query requirements (regulatory reports, analytics dashboards, ML features) can be satisfied by adding new projections that consume the same event stream. No changes to the write model required. New projections can be bootstrapped by replaying historical events."},{"area":"Event schema evolution","evolution_path":"Use event upcasting to handle schema changes. Old events are transformed to the current schema at read time. This allows the domain model to evolve without migrating historical events. Versioned event types (e.g., ApplicantIncomeUpdated_v2) with upcasters from v1→v2."},{"area":"Integration with external systems","evolution_path":"The event stream can be consumed by new downstream systems (e.g., fraud detection, credit scoring, reporting warehouse) without modifying the core loan application logic. Open Host Service pattern — expose events via a documented API or message broker."},{"area":"Regulatory requirement changes","evolution_path":"New compliance requirements (e.g., 'show me all changes made by a specific employee') can be served by building a new projection without touching the core domain. The historical data is already captured; only the view needs to change."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Event schema (published events)","reason":"Once events are published and stored, their schema becomes a commitment. Changing the meaning of a published event retroactively is extremely dangerous — it would rewrite audit history. Event schemas must be treated as an API contract. Additions are safe; modifications require upcasting; removals are effectively impossible."},{"area":"Aggregate identity and stream partitioning","reason":"Changing how aggregates are identified (e.g., switching from application-level to borrower-level streams) would require migrating the entire event store. This decision is effectively permanent once production data exists."},{"area":"Event store immutability","reason":"By design, the event store does not support UPDATE or DELETE. This is a feature for audit compliance but means that incorrect events (e.g., due to a bug) must be compensated with corrective events, not removed. GDPR right-to-erasure requires crypto-shredding or similar techniques rather than deletion."},{"area":"Event ordering guarantees","reason":"The system assumes ordered event delivery per stream. Changing the event store technology or introducing partitioned consumers must preserve this guarantee or risk projection corruption."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Event schema evolution complexity — as the loan domain evolves, managing dozens of event versions with upcasters becomes a significant maintenance burden. Without discipline, the event schema becomes a legacy code graveyard.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Establish an event schema registry from day one. Use explicit versioning (event_type + version). Write upcasters as pure functions with comprehensive tests. Review event schema changes with the same rigor as public API changes. Consider a schema registry tool (e.g., Avro with schema registry)."},{"risk":"Eventual consistency confusing users — loan officers update an application and don't immediately see the change reflected. In financial services, this can cause duplicate submissions or incorrect decisions.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement read-your-own-writes pattern: after a successful command, return the updated state directly from the command response (or use a causal consistency token that the query service can honor). Alternatively, for critical paths, use synchronous projections that update within the same transaction boundary."},{"risk":"Event store becomes a performance bottleneck — long-lived loan applications with complex histories accumulate large event streams. Aggregate rehydration and projection rebuilds become slow.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Implement snapshotting with configurable frequency (e.g., every 50 events). Monitor aggregate stream sizes. For projection rebuilds, use parallel replay with checkpointing. Set performance budgets: aggregate load must complete in <50ms."},{"risk":"GDPR / data privacy conflict with immutable event store — regulations may require deletion of personal data, which conflicts with immutable append-only storage.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement crypto-shredding: encrypt PII fields in events with a per-borrower encryption key. To 'delete' data, destroy the encryption key. Events remain intact but PII becomes unrecoverable. This satisfies both audit immutability and privacy regulations. Design this in from the start — retrofitting is extremely painful."},{"risk":"Team lacks event sourcing experience — ES/CQRS has a steep learning curve. Incorrect implementation (e.g., mutable events, broken projections, missing idempotency) creates subtle, hard-to-debug issues.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Invest in team training before implementation. Start with a spike/proof-of-concept on a single aggregate. Use a proven event store library (Marten for .NET, Axon for Java, EventStoreDB as dedicated store) rather than building from scratch. Establish patterns and templates early."},{"risk":"Projection rebuild time grows with event volume — as the system accumulates years of loan application events, rebuilding a projection from scratch (needed after bugs or schema changes) may take hours or days.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Maintain projection checkpoints. Design projections to be rebuildable in parallel by aggregate. Archive old events to cold storage with pre-built snapshots. Set an SLA for projection rebuild time and monitor it."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase the implementation. Phase 1: Introduce event sourcing for the loan application aggregate with a proven event store (EventStoreDB or PostgreSQL-backed using a library like Marten/pg-es). Build the core command handler, event persistence, and a single read model that replaces the current query path. Phase 2: Build the audit-specific projection and compliance API. Phase 3: Migrate historical data by generating synthetic events from current loan_application table snapshots (each existing record becomes a LoanApplicationImported event). Phase 4: Decommission the old update-in-place table. Run both systems in parallel during transition with reconciliation checks.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/loan/domain/LoanApplication.aggregate — Aggregate root with event-sourced state, command handlers, business rule validation","src/loan/domain/events/ — Domain event definitions: LoanSubmitted, ApplicantDetailsUpdated, StatusChanged, DocumentAttached, UnderwritingDecisionMade, etc.","src/loan/domain/commands/ — Command definitions with validation rules","src/loan/infrastructure/EventStore — Event store adapter (wraps chosen technology)","src/loan/infrastructure/LoanRepository — Loads/saves aggregates via event stream + snapshots","src/loan/projections/LoanReadModel — Current-state projection for operational queries","src/loan/projections/AuditTrailProjection — Field-level change history and temporal state reconstruction","src/loan/api/LoanCommandController — Command ingestion endpoint","src/loan/api/LoanQueryController — Read model query endpoint","src/compliance/api/AuditController — Compliance-specific query endpoint (point-in-time, change history)","src/shared/events/EventSchema — Event versioning and upcasting framework","src/shared/infrastructure/SnapshotStore — Snapshot persistence and retrieval","db/migrations/ — Event store schema (streams, events, snapshots tables if using relational DB)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use the Decider pattern for aggregate command handling: (state, command) → events and (state, event) → state. This separates decision logic from persistence concerns and makes aggregates trivially testable. Events should be past-tense named (LoanSubmitted not SubmitLoan). Every event must include: aggregateId, version, timestamp, userId (actor), and a strongly-typed payload. Projections must be idempotent — processing the same event twice must produce the same result. Use the Outbox pattern if you need to publish events to external systems with at-least-once delivery guarantees.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT store derived/computed data in events — events should capture facts, not interpretations. Projections compute derived state.","Do NOT use the event store as a message bus — it's a persistence mechanism. If you need pub-sub for external consumers, project events to a message broker (Kafka, RabbitMQ).","Do NOT allow direct database writes that bypass the aggregate — every mutation must go through the command → event → store pipeline or the audit trail has gaps.","Do NOT forget idempotency in projections — at-least-once delivery means projections will see duplicates. Use event position/version as an idempotency key.","Do NOT skip the crypto-shredding design for PII — retrofitting this is a nightmare. Bake it in from event #1.","Do NOT design events that are too granular (one event per field change) or too coarse (one event per command with entire state). Find the semantic middle ground: events should represent meaningful business transitions.","Do NOT let the event store grow unbounded without an archival strategy — financial systems run for decades. Plan for event archival from the start."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Event sourcing is a strong architectural fit for immutable audit trail requirements in financial services","CQRS is necessary to maintain query performance alongside event sourcing","Crypto-shredding is the correct approach for reconciling immutability with data privacy regulations","The aggregate-per-loan-application boundary is correct","Phased migration strategy from update-in-place to event sourcing"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific technology choice (EventStoreDB vs PostgreSQL-backed vs Kafka) depends on existing infrastructure, team expertise, and operational maturity — cannot recommend without knowing the stack","Whether the team has sufficient event sourcing experience to execute this without significant ramp-up time","Volume characteristics (number of loan applications, events per application, query patterns) that would inform snapshotting strategy and projection design","Whether a simpler bi-temporal table approach might satisfy the compliance team's actual needs at 30% of the complexity cost — this depends on how literally they need point-in-time reconstruction vs just a changelog"],"assumptions":["The compliance requirement for point-in-time state reconstruction is strict — they need to see the exact state of every field at any arbitrary timestamp, not just a log of changes","The platform is not purely CRUD — there are meaningful business rules and status transitions that benefit from aggregate modeling","The team is willing to invest in the learning curve and operational complexity of event sourcing","The system will continue to evolve with new compliance and reporting requirements, justifying the upfront investment in event-driven architecture"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Event sourcing is architecturally well-suited for this loan application audit requirement — the compliance needs (immutable history, point-in-time reconstruction, field-level change tracking) are precisely what event sourcing delivers by design. However, event sourcing introduces significant operational and developmental complexity (event versioning, schema evolution, CQRS projections, eventual consistency) that must be weighed against simpler alternatives. My recommendatio
